text: this is a video demonstration of robotic posterior exentration for locally advanced rectal cancer using davinci xi platform the patient is a fifty six year old postmenopausal lady with a mid rectal tumor starting six centimeters from inner verge and infiltrating posterior vaginal wall and cervix she has received neo adjuvant chemo radiation and now plan for posterior exentration four robotic ports are inserted along a straight line which is four centimeters superior and parallel to the line joining right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus

The surgical team performs robotic posterior exenteration for locally advanced rectal cancer using the Da Vinci Xi platform. Four robotic ports are inserted in a straight line above the right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus. The dissection begins with creating a plane beneath the inferior mesenteric vein, extending laterally. The rectal tube is mobilized from medial to lateral, and the inferior mesenteric artery is dissected and divided beyond the left colic artery's origin. The descending colon and splenic flexure are mobilized, followed by posterior rectal mobilization down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon performs abdominal dissection similar to a standard robotic total mesorectal excision. A plane is created underneath the inferior mesenteric vein and extended laterally. The rectal tube is mobilized from medial to lateral, and the inferior mesenteric artery is dissected and divided beyond the origin of the left colic artery. The descending colon and splenic flexure are mobilized, followed by posterior rectal mobilization down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection by addressing the infundibulopelvic ligaments. The peritoneum is divided along the line of TALT without initially opening the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The ureter is identified, and the infundibulopelvic ligament is lifted off the retroperitoneal tissue. Subsequently, the posterior leaf of the broad ligament is divided to isolate the infundibulopelvic ligament, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon begins the lateral dissection by addressing the infundibulopelvic ligaments. The peritoneum is divided along the line of TALT, and the ureter is identified. The infundibulopelvic ligament is lifted off the retroperitoneal tissue. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is then divided to isolate the infundibulopelvic ligament, which is subsequently clipped and divided.
The surgeon fully opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and mobilizes the parametral tissue medially to ensure clear surgical margins.

Following the division of the infundibulopelvic ligament, the surgeon fully opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and mobilizes the parametral tissue medially to ensure clear surgical margins.

The surgeon cauterizes and divides the round ligament, followed by medial mobilization of the ureter to facilitate dissection of the uterine vessels at their root. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced down to its origin by dividing the overlying soft tissue, revealing the origin of the uterine vessels through gentle dissection.

The surgeon cauterizes and divides the round ligament. The ureter is medially mobilized to facilitate dissection of the uterine vessels at their root. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced to its origin by dividing the overlying soft tissue. Distal dissection reveals the origin of the uterine vessels.
The surgeon dissects, clips, and divides both the uterine artery and uterine vein individually, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon individually dissects, clips, and divides the uterine artery and uterine vein, ensuring clear separation of these vascular structures.

The surgeon repeats the dissection steps on the right side, dividing the anterior peritoneum to mobilize the bladder away from the cervix and upper part of the vagina. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament appears very short due to the patient's postmenopausal status.

The surgeon repeats the dissection steps on the right side, then divides the anterior peritoneum to mobilize the bladder away from the cervix and upper part of the vagina. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament appears very short due to the patient's postmenopausal status.

The surgeon performs an anterior colpotomy after mobilizing the anterior vaginal wall, extending the incision laterally. Soft tissue beyond the vaginal walls is cauterized to address the descending uterine vessels and vaginal arteries.

The surgeon performs an anterior colpotomy after mobilizing the anterior vaginal wall. The incision is extended laterally, and the soft tissue beyond the vaginal walls is cauterized to address the descending uterine vessels and vaginal arteries.

The surgeon divides and dissects the posterior vaginal wall away from the anterior wall of the rectum, ensuring safe closure of the vaginal vault and adequate mobilization of the distal rectal stump for colorectal anastomosis.

The surgeon divides the posterior vaginal wall and carefully dissects it away from the anterior wall of the rectum, ensuring safe closure of the vaginal vault and adequate mobilization of the distal rectal stump for colorectal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs distal rectal transection intracorporeally using an endostapler, requiring two firings to complete the process. The specimen, including the rectosigmoid, uterus, vagina, and tubes, is delivered outside through the future ileostomy site. The proximal bowel division is performed extracorporeally.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal distal rectal transection using an endostapler, requiring two firings to complete the cut. The specimen, including the rectosigmoid, uterus, vagina, and tubes, is then delivered outside through the future ileostomy site. The proximal bowel division is performed extracorporeally.
The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using continuous sutures with a violet-colored suture material.
The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using continuous sutures made of vitriol, ensuring a secure closure of the vaginal vault.
The surgeon performs colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, followed by the creation of a diversion ileostomy.
The surgeon performs colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, followed by the creation of a diversion ileostomy.
